steps.voice: Welcome to RDM7. Let's walk through the first-time setup.
On first boot, the dash automatically scans your CAN bus at four common bitrates to find where your ECU is broadcasting.
Scan complete. It found CAN traffic at 500 kilobits — the most common automotive rate.
Tap Apply and Continue to lock that bitrate in.
Step two: pick your ECU. Pre-loaded presets cover Haltech, Link, MoTeC, MaxxECU, MS3-Pro, and Ford Falcon.
Each preset carries its own CAN signal map, so the default layout's panels bind to the right signals automatically.
Haltech Elite is pre-selected here. Tap Apply.
Step three: how to connect to the web editor.
Option one, recommended: join your shop or home WiFi. Once connected, the dash shows its IP address in Device Settings and you browse to the editor from any laptop or phone on that network.
Option two: plug a USB cable into your laptop and the RDM Desktop Studio picks it up automatically — no WiFi needed.
Option three is a fallback: the dash hosts its own hotspot. Join that directly from your phone when nothing else is available.
Let's set up WiFi now. Tap Join a WiFi Network.
This is the full WiFi Settings page — the same one you reach later from Device Settings.
On the left, Mode switches between Off, WiFi client, and Hotspot. The Start on Boot dropdown below it remembers what mode to come up in next power-on.
On the right, nearby networks are listed with signal strength. Let's pick Home 2.4.
Connected. The dash's IP now appears in Device Settings so you can open the web editor from anywhere on that network.
And this is what you've just set up — your ECU's live CAN data rendered through the default layout.
RPM at the top, speed and gear in the centre, coolant and throttle bars at the bottom, eight signal panels on each side. Every widget you see here can be moved, resized, or rebound in the web editor.
That's the full first-boot flow. You're ready to drive.
